This is the brief on carbon and its compounds. You know, while carbon makes up practically everything around us, from our bodies to the food we eat, it's actually surprisingly scarce on Earth, which makes it a pretty bizarre chemical paradox. First, it's the fundamental building block of all living structures. Think of carbon as the ultimate, incredibly versatile lego brick of the universe.

Even though there's only a tiny amount of it in the Earth's crust and atmosphere, it somehow manages to build endless, complex structures, like long chains, branches, and rings. So how exactly does such a rare element build so many different things? Second, the secret really lies in covalent bonding. Carbon has an atomic number of six, meaning it has exactly four outer electrons.

Now, you might be wondering, why doesn't it just steal or give away electrons like other elements do? Well, gaining four would totally overwhelm its nucleus and losing four takes way too much energy. So it simply shares them with other atoms to form what we call covalent bonds. So what does this sharing actually mean for the physical objects around us?

Finally, let's look at its physical traits. While those shared bonds inside the molecule are super strong, the forces between different molecules are surprisingly weak. It's kind of like a group of people holding hands really tightly, but totally ignoring the group standing right next to them. Because of these weak outer forces, carbon compounds generally have low melting and boiling points.

And since those shared electrons are locked up in that tight handshake, they are really poor conductors of electricity. Ultimately, carbon's simple little trick of sharing electrons is the literal foundation of all biological complexity on Earth.
